name: Tantalus
description: Tantalus is a figure of enduring infamy in Greek mythology, primarily known for his egregious transgression against the gods and the eternal punishment he subsequently endured. He was a son of Zeus (though some accounts name his father as Tmolus) and the nymph Pluto, and ruled over a region in Phrygia or Lydia. Tantalus was initially favored by the gods, invited to dine at their table and privy to their secrets. However, he abused this privilege, either by stealing nectar and ambrosia to share with mortals, or, more famously, by testing the gods' omniscience. He infamously murdered his son Pelops, cooked him, and served him to the gods. This act of cannibalism and profound disrespect for divine authority resulted in his eternal torment in the underworld. The nature of his punishment is threefold: he stands in a pool of water beneath a fruit tree, both always just out of reach, symbolizing insatiable desire and eternal frustration.
subtitle: The Tormented One

keyMyths.title: The Feast of Tantalus
keyMyths.description: Tantalus, seeking to test the gods' all-knowing nature, slaughtered his son Pelops, dismembered him, and cooked him into a stew. He then served this meal to the gods during a banquet. The gods, with the exception of Demeter, who was distracted by the loss of her daughter Persephone and unknowingly consumed a shoulder, immediately recognized the heinous nature of the dish and refused to partake. This act of cannibalism and deceit sealed Tantalus's fate, condemning him to eternal punishment in the underworld.

keyMyths.title: The Theft of Nectar and Ambrosia
keyMyths.description: Another version of Tantalus's transgression involves the theft of nectar and ambrosia from the gods' table. He not only consumed these divine substances himself, but also shared them with mortals, thus revealing the secrets of immortality and divine sustenance to those not meant to possess such knowledge. This act of betrayal and breach of divine trust further solidified his reputation as a figure of profound disrespect and sacrilege.

keyMyths.title: Tantalus's Punishment
keyMyths.description: Tantalus's punishment in the underworld is one of the most iconic and evocative images of eternal torment in Greek mythology. He stands in a pool of clear water, yet whenever he bends to drink, the water recedes, leaving him perpetually thirsty. Above him hang branches laden with luscious fruits, but whenever he reaches to pluck one, the branches sway out of his grasp, leaving him perpetually hungry. This agonizing cycle of unfulfilled desire and eternal frustration perfectly embodies the consequences of his hubris and his violation of divine law.

sources.author: Apollodorus
sources.description: A comprehensive compendium of Greek myths and legends, providing a detailed account of Tantalus's lineage and crimes.
sources.text: A comprehensive compendium of Greek myths and legends, providing a detailed account of Tantalus's lineage and crimes.

sources.author: Homer
sources.description: Features a description of Tantalus's punishment in the underworld, highlighting the eternal frustration of unfulfilled desires.

sources.author: Hyginus
sources.description: Offers alternative versions of Tantalus's transgressions, including the theft of nectar and ambrosia from the gods.
